citations.desc: Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors).
citations.narrative:
Two animals were identified during the inspection as requiring veterinary care. The facility did not identify the veterinary
issues prior to the inspection.
One white female goat had overgrown hoofs on both the front and hind feet. The hoof claws were grown approximately 2
inches from the wall of the hoof on the toes of the hind feet and were splayed outward. The animal did not have an
abnormal gait.
One white female haired sheep (Kathy) had overgrown hoofs on both the front and hind feet. The Hoof claws were
splayed outward and excessively long. The back left hoof had a crack in the claw that caused the approximately one-inch
tip of the claw to be pointed inward. The animal did not have an abnormal gait.
Failure to promptly identify hoof abnormalities can delay treatment and may cause undue pain and distress in animals
with such conditions.
Correct by ensuring that the animals listed above, and the condition of those animals are reported to the attending
veterinarian so that the animals can be properly examined, diagnosed, and treated. Additionally, ensure that daily
observation of animals is adequate to identify any signs of poor health. Ensure that there is accurate and timely
communication of problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being to the attending veterinarian. Correct by, 28-May-
2026.
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a Facility Representative.
n

narrative:
2.40(b)(3) 
   
Attending veterinarian and adequate veterinary care (dealers and exhibitors). 
Two animals were identified during the inspection as requiring veterinary care. The facility did not identify the veterinary 
issues prior to the inspection. 
 
One white female goat had overgrown hoofs on both the front and hind feet. The hoof claws were grown approximately 2 
inches from the wall of the hoof on the toes of the hind feet and were splayed outward. The animal did not have an 
abnormal gait.  
 
One white female haired sheep (Kathy) had overgrown hoofs on both the front and hind feet. The Hoof claws were 
splayed outward and excessively long. The back left hoof had a crack in the claw that caused the approximately one-inch 
tip of the claw to be pointed inward. The animal did not have an abnormal gait.  
 
Failure to promptly identify hoof abnormalities can delay treatment and may cause undue pain and distress in animals 
with such conditions.  
 
Correct by ensuring that the animals listed above, and the condition of those animals are reported to the attending 
veterinarian so that the animals can be properly examined, diagnosed, and treated. Additionally, ensure that daily 
observation of animals is adequate to identify any signs of poor health. Ensure that there is accurate and timely 
communication of problems of animal health, behavior, and well-being to the attending veterinarian. Correct by, 28-May-
2026. 
 
This inspection and exit interview were conducted with a Facility Representative.
